data.content:
The Course
The course covers various topics including information retrieval, search engines, recommender systems, and large language models.

Late Assignment Policy
The Late Assignment Policy stipulates that any late assignment will receive a grade of 0.

Course Schedule
A rough schedule for the course, subject to change, outlining topics and activities per week.<SEP>The Course Schedule provides a rough outline of weekly topics and important dates, which is subject to change.

Boolean Retrieval
A course topic covered during Week 1.<SEP>Boolean Retrieval is a topic covered in Week 1 of the course.

AI Policy
The AI Policy allows submission of AI-generated code if properly documented with the prompt and significant parts of the response, while emphasizing student responsibility for error identification and improvement.

Vector Space Model
A course topic covered during Week 2.<SEP>Vector Space Model is a topic covered in Week 2 of the course.

TF-IDF
A course topic covered during Week 2.<SEP>TF-IDF is a topic covered in Week 2 of the course.

Course Overview
An introductory topic covered during Week 1 of the course.<SEP>Course Overview is the topic covered in Week 1 of the course.

BM25
A course topic covered during Week 2.<SEP>BM25 is a topic covered in Week 2 of the course.

Link Analysis
A course topic covered during Week 3.<SEP>A topic covered in Week 3 of the course schedule.

Learning to Rank
A course topic covered during Week 5.<SEP>A topic covered in Week 5 of the course schedule.

Recommender Systems (Bayesian Personalized Ranking)
A topic on recommender systems, focusing on Bayesian personalized ranking, covered in Week 7 of the course schedule.

Recommender Systems (Content-Based)
A topic on recommender systems, focusing on content-based methods, covered in Week 6 of the course schedule.

Recommender Systems (Collaborative Filtering)
A topic on recommender systems, focusing on collaborative filtering, covered in Week 6 of the course schedule.

Recommender Systems (Matrix Factorization)
A topic on recommender systems, focusing on matrix factorization, covered in Week 7 of the course schedule.

Word2vec
A topic on word embeddings, covered in Week 8 of the course schedule.

Evaluation of Search Engines
A course topic covered during Week 4.<SEP>A topic covered in Week 4 of the course schedule.

CSCE 670
CSCE 670 is the official course for Information Storage and Retrieval, Section 600 (46627), offered in Spring 2026 at College Station, with 3 credit hours (3-0).

Information Storage and Retrieval (Topic)
This topic covers the representation, storage, and access to very large multimedia document collections, fundamental data structures and algorithms, and techniques to design and evaluate complete retrieval systems, including indexing, compressing, and querying large collections.

Yu Zhang
Yu Zhang is the instructor for CSCE 670, with an email yuzhang@tamu.edu, office at PETR 222, and office hours on Mondays 3:30 – 4:30 PM.

CSCE 670 Spring 2026 Semester
The CSCE 670 course is offered during the Spring 2026 semester, starting January 12, 2026, and ending May 05, 2026.

CSCE 670 Meeting Schedule
Lectures for CSCE 670 are held on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays from 1:50 PM to 2:40 PM in Meeting Location HRBB 113.

CSCE 310
CSCE 310 is a prerequisite course for CSCE 670.

CSCE 603
CSCE 603 is a prerequisite course for CSCE 670.

Instructor Approval (CSCE 670)
Approval of the instructor is an alternative prerequisite for CSCE 670.

Graduate Classification (CSCE 670)
Graduate classification is a prerequisite for CSCE 670.

CSCE 670 Web Search Learning Outcome
Upon completion of CSCE 670, learners will be able to define and explain key concepts and models relevant to web search, including text indexing, retrieval models, evaluation, Web crawling, link-based algorithms like PageRank, and learning to rank.

CSCE 670 System Design Learning Outcome
Upon completion of CSCE 670, learners will be able to design, implement, and evaluate the core algorithms underlying a fully functional web search system and recommendation system.

CSCE 670 Recommender Systems Learning Outcome
Upon completion of CSCE 670, learners will be able to define and explain key concepts and models relevant to recommender systems, including collaborative filtering, matrix factorization, recommender system evaluation, and implicit recommendation.

CSCE 670 Research Application Learning Outcome
Upon completion of CSCE 670, learners will be able to identify the salient features and apply recent research results in web search and recommender systems, including adversarial information retrieval and neural models of retrieval and recommendation.

Mining of Massive Datasets
Mining of Massive Datasets is an optional textbook for CSCE 670, authored by Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, and Jeffrey D. Ullman, published in 2014, and available online.

Introduction to Information Retrieval
Introduction to Information Retrieval is an optional textbook for CSCE 670, authored by Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan, and Hinrich Schutze, published in 2009, and available online.

Jure Leskovec
Jure Leskovec is an author of the textbook "Mining of Massive Datasets."

Jeffrey D. Ullman
Jeffrey D. Ullman is an author of the textbook "Mining of Massive Datasets."

Anand Rajaraman
Anand Rajaraman is an author of the textbook "Mining of Massive Datasets."

Christopher D. Manning
Christopher D. Manning is one of the authors of the textbook "Retrieval".<SEP>Christopher D. Manning is an author of the textbook "Introduction to Information Retrieval."

Prabhakar Raghavan
Prabhakar Raghavan is one of the authors of the textbook "Retrieval".<SEP>Prabhakar Raghavan is an author of the textbook "Introduction to Information Retrieval."

Hinrich Schutze
Hinrich Schutze is one of the authors of the textbook "Retrieval".<SEP>Hinrich Schutze is an author of the textbook "Introduction to Information Retrieval."

CSCE 670 Grading Scale
The grading scale for CSCE 670 defines letter grades based on percentage ranges: A (>=90%), B (>=80% and < 90%), C (>=70% and < 80%), D (>=60% and < 70%).

Retrieval
Retrieval is a textbook written by Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan, and Hinrich Schutze, published in 2009, and available online at https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book/.

Course Grading Policy
The Course Grading Policy defines the percentage ranges for letter grades A, B, C, D, and F.

Quizzes
Quizzes contribute 20% to the overall course grade and consist of four brief in-class quizzes.

Homework
Homework contributes 30% to the overall course grade and consists of five programming assignments.

Final
The Final exam contributes 30% to the overall course grade and is a comprehensive final exam.

Group Project
The Group Project contributes 20% to the overall course grade and involves students working in teams on a mini-research project.

Course Homework Assignments
There will be five programming assignments, all in Python, with specific weights (2%, 7%, 7%, 7%, 7%). The first assignment is introductory, focusing on Jupyter Notebook and Python operations.

Course Final Exam
A comprehensive final exam will be held on May 4 from 3:30 PM - 5:30 PM in HRBB 113.

Course Quizzes
There will be four brief in-class quizzes, each contributing 5% to the grade.

Course Group Project
Students will work in teams of 3 or 4 to engage in a mini-research project.

Course Regrade Policy
Students have seven days after receiving a graded assignment to request a regrade in writing, with the instructor reserving the right to regrade the entire assignment.

Course Homework Collaboration Policy
Homework assignments are individual; students should write their own code but are encouraged to discuss approaches and methods. Pair programming, sharing code, or working through specific problems in code together are inappropriate, and all help must be acknowledged.

Course Late Work Policy
This policy states that makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy, referencing Student Rule 7.

Student Rule 7
Student Rule 7 is an academic policy related to excused absences and makeup work.

Course Homework Late Day Policy
Homeworks are due by 11:59 PM on the due date. Each student receives five total late days, with each late day being an indivisible 24-hour unit. After using all late days, late assignments will receive a grade of 0.

Week 1
The first week of the course schedule, covering Course Overview and Boolean Retrieval.

Week 2
The second week of the course schedule, covering TF-IDF, Vector Space Model, and BM25.

Week 3
The third week of the course schedule, covering Link Analysis.

Week 4
The fourth week of the course schedule, covering Evaluation of Search Engines.

Week 5
The fifth week of the course schedule, covering Learning to Rank.

Week 6
The sixth week of the course schedule, covering Recommender Systems (Content-Based; Collaborative Filtering).

Week 7
The seventh week of the course schedule, without specific topics mentioned.

Recommender Systems
A course topic covering Content-Based and Collaborative Filtering, discussed during Week 6.

Content-Based Filtering
A sub-topic of Recommender Systems.

Jupyter Notebook
A tool or concept that students will get familiar with in the first programming assignment.

Collaborative Filtering
A sub-topic of Recommender Systems.

Python Operations
Commonly used programming operations in the course, introduced in the first programming assignment.

May 4
The date on which the comprehensive final exam will be held.

3:30 PM - 5:30 PM
The time slot for the comprehensive final exam.

HRBB 113
The location where the comprehensive final exam will be held.
